title: practical english usage - Part 178
content:
Either you'll leave this house or I'll call the police, (clauses) He's either in London or in New York, (prepositional phrases) You can have either tea or coffee, (nouns) follow either and or.

We often balance this structure, so that the same kind of words or expressions If you want ice cream there's either raspberry, lemon or vanilla.

You can either come with me now or walk home.

/ don't speak either Russian or Polish.

We   use either... or io talk about a choice between two or more possibilities.

2 either      ...  or but not He can both borrow the house and (he can) use the car. (          Both he can Both cannot begin a complete clause in this structure.

/ both play the piano and the violin.

She both dances and she sings, (both + verb; and + clause) prefer to avoid them.

However, unbalanced sentences with both ... and are common. Some people She both dances and sings, (verbs) I spoke to both the Director and her secretary, (nouns) She's both pretty and clever, (adjectives) grammar •  229  subordinate clauses: some general points For punctuation in relative clauses, ►    234.

When we opened the door(,) we got a shock.

When we opened the door, we saw water running down the stairs.

comma  unless the sentence is very short.

When  a subordinate clause begins a sentence, it is more often separated by a had won the election and settled in with a comfortable majority.

They made a large number of disturbing changes in their policies, after they They changed all their policies after they had won the election.

pairs of clauses are often connected without commas. Compare: Commas are often used to separate longer or more complicated clauses. Shorter 2  punctuation For the position of other kinds of clause, ►    Sections 22-25.

Anna said nothing because she was too angry to speak.

- Because she was too angry to speak, Anna said nothing.

She decided to buy the bicycle although it was expensive.

- Although the bicycle was expensive, she decided to buy it.

Just let me know if you need help.

- If you need help, Just let me know.

I slipped while I was taking a shower. (emphasises when it happened) - While I was taking a shower, I slipped. (emphasises what happened) the focus. Putting a clause at the end can give it more importance.

Adverbial clauses can usually go either first or last in a sentence, depending on 1  position      of  subordinate         clauses have some problems in using English conjunctions correctly.

conjunctions in similar ways. However, students who  speak other languages may In most languages of European origin, clauses are joined together by 229 subordinate         clauses:     some    general      poi  nts We don't only go there in winter. We go in summer  too.

are generally preferred.

In informal English not only... but also is not very common; other structures Not only do they need clothing but they are also short of water.

Not only has she been late three times; she has also done no work.

(for more about this word order, ►    270). But can be left out in this case.

followed by auxiliary verb + subject; do is used if there is no other auxiliary Not only can be moved to the beginning of a clause for emphasis. It is then She not only plays the piano, but also the violin.

She not only sings like an angel, but also dances divinely.

Mid-position with the verb (►   200) is also possible.

The place was not only cold, but also damp.

Not only the bathroom was flooded, but also the rest of the house.

Conjunctions, Sentences and ClausesSection 20 grammar •  229  subordinate clauses: some general points She didn't write when I was ill, or when I got married.

We came back because we ran out of money, and because Anna got ill.

However, we  can use and or or together with a repeated conjunction.

(\oi As you know,-that 1 work very hard.) You know that I work very hard.

-   As you know, I work very hard.

(    Because I liked him,  so I tried to Mp  him.) I liked him,  so I tried to help him.

- Because I liked him,  I tried to help him.
